title: BPSC 2026 — Bihar Civil Services Exam
meta_description: BPSC 2026 — Bihar Public Service Commission exam for SDM, DSP, CO. Prelims pattern, syllabus, salary ₹56K–2L/mo, age limit, and preparation tips.

hero.one_liner: Bihar's state civil services exam — become SDM, DSP, Circle Officer, or District Officer through BPSC Combined Competitive Exam (72nd CCE)

what_is_it: BPSC (Bihar Public Service Commission) Combined Competitive Examination is Bihar's state-level civil services exam — equivalent to UPSC CSE for Bihar state administration. The 72nd BPSC Prelims are scheduled for July 26, 2026, with notification expected in April 2026. It recruits for Group A and Group B administrative, police, and revenue posts: Sub-Divisional Officer (SDM), Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP), Circle Officer (CO), District Transport Officer, Assistant Registrar (Revenue), and 20+ other specialized posts in Bihar government.

BPSC has three evaluation stages: Preliminary Exam (150 MCQs, General Studies only, NO CSAT — a major advantage), Main Exam (4 papers: 2 GS papers, 1 optional, 1 Hindi qualifying), and Interview (120 marks). The exam pattern is simpler than UPSC CSE — Prelims has only ONE paper (no CSAT burden), and candidates can skip advanced aptitude testing. This advantage makes BPSC more accessible for many aspirants.

Recent cycles show intense competition: BPSC 69th (latest completed) received 6+ lakh applications for approximately 900 vacancies. However, the exam difficulty is lower than UPSC CSE, and the no-negative-marking policy in recent Prelims cycles (67th-69th) makes the exam less penalizing. The 72nd BPSC (2026) is expected to continue this trend.

Starting salary at Pay Level 10 is ₹56,100 basic + DA (currently ~50%) + HRA + allowances = ₹80,000-1,00,000/month in-hand. Bihar's cost of living is significantly lower than Delhi/metro cities, making this salary provide an excellent quality of life. BPSC officers are highly respected in Bihar society and exercise significant administrative power at district/sub-division levels, directly affecting governance in India's most populous state.

key_details.label: Education
key_details.value: Bachelor's degree from any recognized university (any stream). Final year students eligible.

key_details.label: Age Limit
key_details.value: 20-37 years (General). OBC/Women: 40, SC/ST: 42. Relaxation up to 5 years for reserved categories.

key_details.label: Attempts
key_details.value: Unlimited attempts — only age limit matters. Attempt every cycle until you reach upper age limit.

key_details.label: Prelims
key_details.value: ONE paper only: General Studies (150 Qs, 2 hours, 150 marks). NO CSAT — no negative marking in recent cycles.

key_details.label: Selection
key_details.value: Prelims → Mains (4 papers) → Interview (120 marks) → Final Merit (Mains + Interview only)

exam_pattern.description: 150 MCQs on General Studies — history, geography, polity, economy, science, current affairs, and Bihar-specific topics. This is a qualifying round. No CSAT in BPSC is a major advantage over UPSC. Recent cycles (67th, 68th, 69th) had NO negative marking; verify for 72nd CCE.
exam_pattern.negative_marking: No negative marking in recent BPSC Prelims (confirm for 72nd cycle)

exam_pattern.description: Descriptive papers. GS Paper 1 & 2 (300+300 = 600 marks), Optional Subject (100 marks), Hindi Qualifying (100 marks, need 30% to qualify). Total merit determined by Mains + Interview only.

subjects.name: GS Paper 1: History, Polity, Geography, Science
GS Paper 2: Economy, Bihar-specific, Current Affairs

exam_pattern.duration: 4 papers, typically 2-3 hours each, over 3-4 consecutive days
exam_pattern.negative_marking: No negative marking in descriptive papers

major_posts.department: Revenue Department — land records, land disputes

extra_sections.heading: Bihar-Specific GS Preparation — Critical Topics & Resources
content: BPSC gives 15-25% weightage to Bihar topics in Prelims and even more in Mains. Most UPSC-focused coaching institutes skip Bihar content, giving BPSC-specific aspirants an edge.
Dedicate 20% of your GS study time to Bihar.

Ancient: Magadha empire, Maurya dynasty (Ashoka's empire centered in Patna/Pataliputra), Nalanda University (oldest university 5th-12th century), Vikramashila University (8th-12th century Buddhist learning center). Medieval: Sher Shah Suri (Afghan ruler who defeated Humayun, reformed administration from Bihar), Mughals (administrative reforms).

Modern: Champaran Satyagraha (1917 — Gandhi's first major movement), Khilafat Movement participation, Quit India Movement (1942), Ram Mohan Roy's Brahmo Samaj role, role of Bihar in independence struggle.
River systems: Ganga (main), Sone (tributary), Kosi (flood-prone), Gandak, Mahananda. Ganga's flood management is critical — study Kosi embankment breaches (2008).

Agricultural zones: North Bihar (wheat, makhana — Bihar is world's largest makhana producer), South Bihar (sugarcane, rice). Mineral resources: Mica (world's largest producer historically — now declining), coal (through former Jharkhand part).

State GDP, agricultural dependence (70% population in rural areas), key industries (sugar, textiles, mining). Major government schemes: Bihar's flagship programs (check Bihar government website for latest).
Infrastructure projects: Highway expansions, Bullet train corridor feasibility, smart cities initiatives.
Books: 'Bihar — Ek Parichay' (Hindi) by Imtiaz Ahmed, NCERT books for Bihar history. News sources: Dainik Bhaskar (Bihar edition), Amar Ujala (North India focus).
Websites: Official Bihar government website, BPSC notification PDFs (read carefully for state-specific references).

extra_sections.heading: BPSC vs UPSC vs UPPSC — Comparison for Aspirants
rows: GS + CSAT (250 Qs) — Paper 1 weighted more
Bihar domiciled aspirants, lower CSAT anxiety
UP domiciled, slightly easier than UPSC

preparation_tips: BPSC Prelims has NO CSAT and NO negative marking (recent cycles) — this is a massive advantage. Attempt ALL 150 questions without fear. Unlike UPSC where skipping 20 questions saves you 5-7 marks in negative marking, BPSC rewards attempting all. Focus on speed + accuracy over selectivity.

Bihar-specific GS is your competitive edge. Dedicate 1-2 hours weekly to Bihar topics: read Dainik Bhaskar Bihar edition, follow Bihar government announcements, study Bihar-specific NCERT content. Most UPSC-only candidates neglect this — use it as your differentiator.

Choose an optional subject wisely for Mains. BPSC has a focused optional list. Popular high-scoring options: Hindi Literature (advantage for Hindi-medium students), History, Political Science, Public Administration. Choose based on (1) overlap with GS, (2) availability of Hindi study material, (3) past performance in the subject.

Answer writing for BPSC is more direct and factual than UPSC. BPSC evaluators prefer: clear, concise, point-wise answers with relevant data → avoid lengthy philosophical discussions. Practice writing answers in point format with examples from Bihar (SDM answers should reflect local governance realities).

faqs.question: BPSC has no negative marking — is this definitely true for 72nd CCE?
faqs.answer: Recent cycles (67th, 68th, 69th) had no negative marking. However, BPSC can change policy for future cycles. Always verify in the official 72nd CCE notification. Even if negative marking is introduced, it's typically -1/3 or -1/4 — less severe than UPSC (-1/3). Check bpsc.bih.nic.in once notification releases.

faqs.question: Can non-Bihar residents appear for BPSC?
faqs.answer: Yes, absolutely. BPSC is open to all Indian citizens regardless of residence. However, some posts (particularly reserved category positions) may require Bihar domicile. For general category open posts, anyone from any state can appear and be selected. No domicile restriction for general candidates.

faqs.question: How difficult is BPSC compared to UPPSC and UPSC?
faqs.answer: Difficulty ranking: UPSC CSE > UPPSC ≈ BPSC. BPSC Prelims is easier (no CSAT) but Mains has Bihar-specific topics. Effective competition is lower for BPSC (6 lakh for 900 posts) vs UPSC (13 lakh for 1000 posts). Clearing UPSC Prelims means you're competitive for BPSC. Many UPSC aspirants use BPSC as practice/safety net.

faqs.question: What are recommended sources for Bihar current affairs?
faqs.answer: Primary: (1) Dainik Bhaskar Bihar edition — read political news, government announcements, development projects, (2) Amar Ujala — North India coverage including Bihar, (3) Monthly magazines: Pratiyogita Darpan (state-specific section), (4) YouTube channels focused on BPSC (check for Bihar news updates), (5) Bihar government official website and press releases.

faqs.question: Is unlimited attempts at BPSC a good strategy?
faqs.answer: Not recommended. While BPSC allows unlimited attempts (only age matters), spend max 3-4 serious, well-prepared attempts. If not clearing within 3-4 years of dedicated preparation, reassess your strategy: either shift focus to other exams (SSC CGL, UGC NET for teaching) or diversify your career path. Continuous repeated failures affect morale and opportunity cost of your time.

faqs.question: SDM vs DSP in BPSC — which post is better?
faqs.answer: Both are at same Pay Level 10 with identical salary. Choice depends on: (1) SDM (revenue) — more administrative responsibility, civil matters, land disputes, disaster management, (2) DSP (police) — law enforcement, criminal cases, field operations. SDM is considered slightly more prestigious (manages district revenue), but police work has more direct societal impact. Choose based on interest: administration vs law enforcement.

faqs.question: Can I transition from BPSC to UPSC later?
faqs.answer: No. BPSC recruitment is for state-level service (Bihar cadre). You cannot directly transition to All-India Services (IAS/IPS) after BPSC selection. However, after 12-15 years of excellent BPSC service, you might be eligible for special All-India Service promotion (rare, merit-based). Or you can resign and reappear for UPSC CSE as a civilian candidate.

syllabus.subject: General Studies Paper I
syllabus.topics: Indian history, geography, polity, economics. Bihar-specific: Bihar history (Maurya, Gupta, Pala dynasties, Champaran Satyagraha), Bihar geography (rivers, soil, minerals), Bihar caste dynamics, Bihar government schemes, Patna University history

syllabus.subject: CSAT / General Aptitude (Qualifying)
syllabus.topics: Comprehension, logical reasoning, analytical ability, basic numeracy, data interpretation, decision making

syllabus.subject: Bihar Specific GK
syllabus.topics: Bihar history (Maurya, Gupta, Pala dynasties, Champaran Satyagraha), Bihar geography (rivers, soil, minerals), Bihar caste dynamics, Bihar government schemes, Patna University history

books.title: Bihar State GK
books.author: Bihar GK by Crown Publications, Lucent GK + Bihar special
books.subject: Bihar GK

study_plan.tasks: Read NCERTs for History, Geography, Polity. Start Bihar state GK book. Study state-specific history, geography, culture, and current schemes. Daily newspaper reading for current affairs.
Laxmikanth Polity, Ramesh Singh Economy. Deep dive into Bihar-specific topics — this is what separates State PSC from UPSC. Start answer writing practice for Mains (250 words).
Solve last 10 years BPSC papers. Weekly full mocks. Focus on Bihar current affairs and state government policies. Interview preparation alongside.
